data.title: GE Healthcare drops as UBS cuts to Sell on valuation
data.description:
GE HealthCare (GEHC) traded lower on Thursday after UBS downgraded GE’s (GE) healthcare spinoff to Sell from Neutral, arguing that the company at its current valuation doesn’t adequately reflect its risks.

Analyst Graham Doyle noted that the Chicago, Illinois-based medical equipment maker, trading less than 10% below its all-time high, is significantly outperforming its peers due to investor enthusiasm over its tariff mitigation efforts and new products such as its Flyrcado diagnostic agent.

“Whilst these are clear positives, we feel the shares and consensus capture their best possible execution,” Doyle wrote, adding that UBS has set EBIT estimates for the company at 0%-7% below consensus, assuming “more gradual product ramps.”

The analyst cited additional competitive headwinds that could further hurt his estimates, including increased rivalry from Chinese players and the recent launch of a U.S. competitor targeting the contrast agent Omnipaque, which accounts for 9% of GEHC’s EBIT.

While the company can meet expectations for Q4 and the 2026 outlook, “we think delivering 2026 will be a challenge, while the Flyrcado launch, China competition, and Omnipaque competition risks could materialize in H2,” Doyle wrote. The analyst indicated a 12-month price target of $77 for GEHC compared to $73 previously.

data.title: Paramount fails to get its suit against Warner Bros. expedited: report
data.description:
A Delaware judge refused to expedite Paramount Skydance Corp.’s (PSKY) lawsuit that accused directors of Warner Bros. Discovery Inc. (WBD) of misleading investors about a more than $82.7B buyout bid from Netflix Inc. (NFLX), Bloomberg reported on Thursday.

According to the report, Judge Morgan Zurn found that Paramount’s demands didn’t meet legal tests for fast-tracking a hearing into accusations that Warner Bros.’ board made erroneous disclosures about the deal.

Developing story…

data.title: Wells Fargo upgrades Broadcom, Analog Devices and Monolithic Power Systems to Overweight
data.description:
Wells Fargo upgraded Broadcom (AVGO), Analog Devices (ADI) and Monolithic Power Systems (MPWR) to Overweight from Equal-weight.

Shares of these three were up 2.6%, 1.5% and 4%, respectively, during early market action on Thursday.

The financial firm edged up Broadcom's price target to $430 from $410.

"We think the recent pull-back in shares of Broadcom, coupled with increasing confidence in potentially meaningful incremental catalysts looking through 2026, gives us the opportunity to get constructive," said Wells Fargo analysts, led by Aaron Rakers, in an in-depth investor report on Thursday.

They highlighted Broadcom's key partnership with Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) in developing its tensor processing units. 

"In addition to Broadcom's announcement that Anthropic (ANTHRO) has ordered $21B in Google TPUs + rack-scale systems, we think indications of potential Google TPU deployments with Meta (META), xAI (X.AI), Apple (AAPL), SSI, and others would be potentially meaningful incremental upside catalysts," Rakers noted.

Meanwhile, Wells Fargo hiked its price target on Analog Devices to $340 from $265.

"With ADI continuing to report positive bookings activity across key growth end markets and signs of a broad market recovery, we expect the company to deliver modest upside to consensus estimates that currently reflect relatively seasonal growth throughout F2026," Rakers said.

Analog Devices reported 40% year-over-year growth during 2025 in its automated test equipment segment. Momentum is expected to continue in 2026 due to leading edge logic and high-bandwidth memory demand.

Finally, the bank inched up Monolithic Power's price target to $1,125 from $1,100.

"Following a reset year in 2025 as NVIDIA (NVDA) expanded its supplier ecosystem, we expect MPWR to deliver accelerating  growth in its Enterprise data segment," Rakers noted. "While we model revenue +39% y/y in 2026, we will be focused on upside drivers as demand visibility improves throughout the year."

data.title: Digital Realty Trust raised to Buy at HSBC on robust 2026 demand outlook
data.description:
Digital Realty Trust (DLR) was raised to Buy from Hold at HSBC Global Investment Research on the back of a robust 2026 demand outlook.

The price target was raised to $193 from $187. Shares were 1.68% higher during Thursday morning trading to $161.94.

The research firm believes that power constraints are still a key factor for the traditional data centers, and oversupply concerns are likely premature for now.

For DLR, the research note said, "We think market concerns on 2027 growth look overdone. We expect 10% revenue and 9% AFFO per share CAGRs 2025e-28e for DLR. Our higher TPs (target price) are driven by rolling forward our valuation base to 2027e (from 2026e), partly offset by lower target multiples."

HSBC's rating aligns with the average sell-side analysts and Seeking Alpha authors ratings of Buy. Meanwhile, the Quant Rating system grades the stock as Hold.

data.title: Citi upgrades Intel, removes Micron from Focus List
data.description:
Investment firm Citi upgraded Intel (INTC) and removed Micron (MU) from its U.S. Focus List on Thursday.

Intel

Intel was raised to Neutral from Sell and put a $50 price target, as the firm believes it should benefit from advanced packaging tightness at Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). In addition, it has a “unique window of opportunity to attract foundry wafer customers with the support of the US government,” analyst Atif Malik said.

“We believe Intel will get AI ASIC spillover demand e.g. TPUs in the back end first and then front-end foundry customers as process yields on 18A-P/14A improve,” Malik added.

Despite the aforementioned positives — as well as the U.S. government being an investor and the prospects of “stabilizing” capex — Intel is not completely out of the woods, Malik added. It is also expected to lose CPU market share to AMD (AMD) and Arm (ARM), and there is the chance that the PC market softens, giving rising memory prices, the analyst posited.

Micron

Micron was removed from the U.S. Focus List, as Malik said the pricing momentum for dynamic random access memory likely “decelerates” in the second-quarter from the first quarter. 

“Micron stock typically follows the [quarter-over-quarter] pricing momentum,” Malik explained. “That said, we expect extended memory [supply-demand] balance through 2026/27 on limited fab space availability and strong AI demand.”

data.title: Tamarack Valley Energy renews normal course issuer bid
data.description:
• Tamarack Valley Energy (TVE:CA) said on Thursday  that the TSX approved its application to renew its normal course issuer bid (NCIB).
  • NCIB would allow the company to repurchase up to 47.7M shares, representing 10% of public float, over 12 months from Jan. 19, 2026. The NCIB will expire by January 18, 2027.
  • The company may repurchase up to 0.41M shares daily, equal to 25% of the average daily trading volume on the TSX over the six months ended Dec. 31, 2025.
  • Under its expiring NCIB, the company was allowed to repurchase up to 51.28M shares between Jan. 19, 2025, and Jan. 18, 2026, and had bought back 34.42M shares at an average price of C$5.01 per share as of Dec. 31, 2025.

data.title: Senate Banking Committee delays its first big vote on crypto bill
data.description:
The U.S. Senate Banking Committee has postponed its markup of its crypto market structure draft bill shortly after Coinbase (COIN) CEO Brian Armstrong condemned the proposed legislation.

Unveiled on Monday, the bill seeks to establish a comprehensive regulatory crypto framework by defining when digital assets are securities or commodities and assigning market oversight of spot crypto markets to the Commodity Futures Trading Commission and securities-based tokens to the Securities and Exchange Commission.

But the first big vote on the draft legislation was delayed late Wednesday and a new date has yet to be set, Tim Scott, chairman of the Senate Banking Committee, said in a statement.

"I’ve spoken with leaders across the crypto industry, the financial sector, and my Democratic and Republican colleagues, and everyone remains at the table working in good faith," Scott said. "This bill reflects months of serious bipartisan negotiations and real input from innovators, investors, and law enforcement. The goal is to deliver clear rules of the road that protect consumers, strengthen our national security, and ensure the future of finance is built in the United States."

His statement came just hours after Coinbase (COIN) CEO Brian Armstrong said that his crypto exchange "can't support" the bill, with "too many issues" as written, including "a defacto ban on tokenized equities," as well as proposed DeFi rules, how it approaches the Commodity Futures trading Commission, and draft changes on the bill.

Meanwhile, crypto prices edged lower in Thursday morning trading, taking a breather from a multi-day rally. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slipped 0.8% in the last 24 hours to $96.2K, while Ether (ETH-USD) edged down 0.3% to $3.34K.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Top and lowest-rated consumer discretionary stocks as Q4 earnings season heats up
data.description:
With Q4 earnings season underway, Seeking Alpha’s Quant ratings has created a list of the top- and bottom-ranked mid- to mega-cap consumer discretionary stocks.

The S&P 500’s Consumer Discretionary sector (NYSEARCA:XLY) fell 0.96% between October 1 and December 31, underperforming the broader S&P 500, which rose 2.0% in the final quarter of 2025.

The sector lagged Health Care (XLV), which gained 7.9%, as well as Financials (XLF) and Materials (XLB), but outperformed Real Estate (XLRE), Utilities (XLU) and Consumer Staples (XLP) during the quarter, as performance across cyclical areas remained uneven.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, the consumer discretionary sector has an average health score of 3.15. The system assigns grades based on quantitative factors such as valuation, earnings growth, profitability, and momentum, with the highest possible score for any company being 5.

The Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) has risen 4.02% year to date and gained 0.12% over the past month.

General Motors (GM) leads the sector with the highest Quant score of 4.96, supported by A+ grades in profitability, momentum and earnings revisions. The automaker has remained in focus during the quarter after posting a third-quarter earnings beat and raising its full-year outlook, while also announcing EV-related cost actions, plant idlings, and workforce reductions.

GM also featured in multiple policy, supply chain and product-related headlines, including tariff mitigation efforts and changes to its EV strategy.

Wayfair (W) follows closely with a Quant score of 4.96, while Garrett Motion (GTX) ranks next with a score of 4.87. PDD Holdings (PDD) and LCI Industries (LCII) round out the top five Quant-rated stocks in the sector, posting scores of 4.83 and 4.78, respectively.

At the lower end of the rankings, Lucid Group (LCID) holds the weakest Quant score in the sector at 1.05, reflecting F grades in both profitability and momentum. The automaker drew attention during the quarter after missing third-quarter earnings expectations, raising new debt to support liquidity and outlining production and delivery targets, while also rolling out new vehicle programs and expansion initiatives.

Stride (LRN), MakeMyTrip (MMYT), VinFast Auto (VFS), and Cava Group (CAVA) also rank among the lowest-rated consumer discretionary stocks, each recording Quant scores below 1.40, highlighting continued pressure on profitability, momentum, and earnings visibility across parts of the sector.

“I expect real estate, financials, and energy sectors to outperform in 2026 as prior underperformance and macro tailwinds set up favorable risk/reward,” an analyst said in a recent report, citing potential benefits from lower interest rates, yield-curve dynamics, and improving sector fundamentals.

The report added that REITs “should benefit from likely rate cuts, attractive yields, and forecasted FFO growth,” while banks could gain from a “steepening yield curve, potential M&A and IPO activity,” and energy stocks may see upside if oil prices revert toward mid-range levels.

data.title: Atlanta Fed's Bostic sees GDP growth exceeding 2% in 2026
data.description:
Atlanta Fed President Raphael Bostic expects U.S. GDP growth at "north of 2%" this year, he said at a Metro Atlanta Chamber event on Thursday.  "Maybe even the mid-2s would not surprise me."

From a historical perspective, that's "incredibly strong."

"What we've seen is an economy that is incredibly resilient," he said. "Economic growth is occurring in this country above long-run potential, and I see that continuing in 2026."

His arguments for that outlook are 1) he expects the resilient consumer to remain resilient and 2) policies from last year's tax bill that encourage businesses to invest and tax breaks for consumers. Furthermore, he said many households' balance sheet are stronger now than they were before the pandemic.

Still, the Fed and policymakers need to keep an eye on the segment of the population that's struggling, he said. The challenge is weighing those two realities.

With that strong economy, the pressures on prices are likely to continue. Moreover, uncertainty around policy that could "create some challenges." In addition, many businesses are not finished incorporating tariffs. He notes that inflation pressures go “far beyond” tariffs, such as in medical insurance.

Bostic reiterated his view that the Fed needs to keep policy restrictive to address higher-than-target inflation. While the labor market has loosened in the past year, a 4.4% unemployment rate is still a strong labor market, he said.

He also stressed the importance of Fed independence, which is particularly salient in light of the Justice Department's probe into Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Independence allows the Fed "the ability to be patient, to not respond to things in the moment that other policymakers might be moved to do," Bostic said. "I think that has served this country well."
